stem:
A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to her general practitioner with widespread bruising and a petechial rash on her shins and palate that her mother first noticed three days ago after a viral illness two weeks before. She has had no nosebleeds, no menorrhagia obviously not applicable, no haematuria, and is eating and playing normally. Her parents are anxious about cancer.

On examination she is well perfused. There is no hepatosplenomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, and no joint swelling. Bruising is scattered without a distribution suggesting trauma. Mucosal bleeding is limited to a few petechiae on the soft palate.

data_table.Examination: scattered cutaneous bruising, palatal petechiae, no hepatosplenomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, no bony tenderness
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 122 g/L (110 to 140); WCC 7.6 x10^9/L with normal differential; platelets 14 x10^9/L (150 to 400); film: isolated thrombocytopenia, no blasts, normal red cells

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine before any management decision
options.rationale: Routine marrow examination is not required in classic acute ITP with a clean film and isolated thrombocytopenia. It is reserved for atypical features, treatment failure, or before steroids in some centres. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric ITP Guidelines)

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg as a single dose with paracetamol pre-medication
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved by HSANZ for active mucosal or visceral bleeding or for raising the count quickly before a procedure. Skin and minor palatal petechiae alone do not justify it. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric ITP Guidelines)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg per day for 14 days followed by a taper
options.rationale: Corticosteroids work in ITP but masking a missed leukaemia would delay diagnosis if the film were not first carefully reviewed. With a clean film, steroids may still be used for mucosal bleeding; cutaneous-only disease does not need them. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric ITP Guidelines)

options.text: Watchful waiting with safety advice, no medication, and clinic review in one week
options.rationale: First-episode acute ITP in a well child with cutaneous bleeding only is managed with observation under the HSANZ and Australian and New Zealand Childhood Haematology Oncology Group consensus. Most resolve within weeks. Treatment is reserved for active mucosal or major bleeding, not for the platelet count alone. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric ITP Guidelines)

options.text: Platelet transfusion 10 mL/kg to raise the count above 50 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusions are ineffective in ITP because the antibody clears infused platelets and are reserved for life-threatening bleeding only. They are not a count-raising intervention here. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric ITP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute ITP in a well child after a viral prodrome with isolated thrombocytopenia, a clean film, no lymphadenopathy, and no organomegaly is managed by observation alone irrespective of the count if bleeding is cutaneous or minor mucosal. Most resolve within 6 weeks. IVIG, anti-D, or steroids are reserved for active mucosal, gastrointestinal, intracranial, or other significant bleeding, or to raise the count rapidly before a procedure. Platelet transfusions are ineffective due to antibody-mediated clearance and reserved for life-threatening bleeding.
key_points: Watchful waiting is HSANZ first-line in well children with cutaneous bleeding only
Treatment is driven by bleeding severity, not by the absolute platelet count
IVIG, anti-D, or corticosteroids for active mucosal or major bleeding
Routine marrow examination is not required in classic acute ITP with a clean film
explanation.pearls: Paediatric ITP: cutaneous-only bleeding is observed; IVIG 0.8 g/kg or prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for active mucosal bleeding. Platelet transfusions are reserved for life-threatening bleeding only.

stem:
A 36-year-old woman attends your general practice for a routine cervical screening test. She has had previous normal screening tests at age 30 and 25; she has no symptoms of abnormal bleeding and is not pregnant. She is up to date with HPV vaccination from school years. She has been with the same partner for 8 years.

The self-collected vaginal swab returns oncogenic HPV (non 16, non 18) positive. A reflex liquid-based cytology from the same sample reports low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL).

data_table.Examination: asymptomatic; speculum examination not performed for self-collected screen
data_table.Investigations: HPV PCR: oncogenic non 16/18 detected; reflex LBC: LSIL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in screening management?

options.text: Refer urgently for colposcopy and directed biopsy within 8 weeks
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy is reserved for oncogenic HPV 16 or 18, for any HPV positive with HSIL on cytology, or persistent intermediate risk on the 12-month repeat. LSIL with non 16/18 HPV does not meet the immediate referral threshold. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines)
source_refs: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months as intermediate risk
options.rationale: Under the NCSP, oncogenic HPV (not 16/18) with reflex LBC of LSIL is intermediate risk. Management is a repeat HPV test in 12 months. Most clear within that interval and avoid unnecessary colposcopy. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines)
source_refs: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test in 5 years as standard low-risk recall
options.rationale: Routine 5-yearly recall applies only to HPV-negative results. Any positive HPV pulls the woman onto either the intermediate risk (12 month repeat) or higher risk (colposcopy) pathway. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines)
source_refs: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Refer for endometrial sampling and a pelvic ultrasound at this visit
options.rationale: Endometrial assessment is indicated for abnormal uterine bleeding or postmenopausal bleeding, not for cervical screening abnormalities. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines)
source_refs: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Prescribe topical imiquimod and recheck cytology in 6 months from now
options.rationale: Imiquimod is for external genital warts, not for cervical screening management. No topical or destructive therapy is indicated for LSIL detected on screening; it is followed by surveillance. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines)
source_refs: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: The Australian NCSP stratifies cervical screening results by HPV status: HPV negative returns to 5-year recall; oncogenic 16/18 goes directly to colposcopy; oncogenic non 16/18 with negative or LSIL cytology becomes intermediate risk and repeats HPV at 12 months; oncogenic non 16/18 with HSIL+ goes to colposcopy. Self-collected vaginal swabs are now accepted as primary screening for HPV detection across the eligible age range. Persistent HPV at the 12-month repeat or any HSIL upgrades to colposcopy.
key_points: Non 16/18 oncogenic HPV with LSIL is intermediate risk: repeat in 12 months
16/18 oncogenic HPV or HSIL on cytology: colposcopy regardless of subtype
HPV negative: return to 5-year recall
Self-collected vaginal swabs are accepted for primary HPV screening across the eligible age range
explanation.pearls: NCSP: 16/18 oncogenic HPV or HSIL cytology to colposcopy; non 16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative or LSIL cytology to repeat HPV at 12 months; HPV negative back to 5-year recall.

sources.label: National Cervical Screening Program Clinical Guidelines

stem:
A 24-year-old woman attends your general practice for advice about irregular periods and growing dark hair on her chin and lower abdomen over the past two years. Her periods come every 50 to 70 days. Her BMI is 28. She is not on contraception and is currently sexually active with a male partner.

Clinical examination confirms terminal hair on the chin, around the umbilicus, and on the lower back. There is no acanthosis nigricans. Pelvic ultrasound was performed last week; the report describes both ovaries containing more than 20 antral follicles 2 to 9 mm in diameter, with normal stromal volume.

data_table.Examination: modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 10, BMI 28 kg/m^2, no virilising features such as clitoromegaly or deepened voice
data_table.Investigations: fasting glucose 5.2 mmol/L (3.9 to 5.4); total testosterone 2.6 nmol/L (less than 2.0); SHBG 28 nmol/L; prolactin 280 mIU/L (less than 500); TSH 1.8 mIU/L; 17-hydroxyprogesterone within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in confirming the diagnosis?

options.text: Order serum DHEAS, androstenedione, and a 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: Further androgen workup is reserved for severe or rapidly progressive hyperandrogenism, virilisation, or testosterone well above the female reference. Her testosterone is only mildly raised and CAH is already excluded. (Source: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

options.text: Repeat pelvic ultrasound in 3 months to confirm ovarian morphology
options.rationale: A single representative ultrasound consistent with PCOS morphology suffices. The threshold under the 2023 update is over 20 follicles per ovary on high-frequency transvaginal scanning. (Source: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

options.text: Diagnose PCOS and counsel on lifestyle plus long-term metabolic risk
options.rationale: She meets the Rotterdam criteria (2018 International PCOS Guideline endorsed by the NHMRC and Monash Centre for Health Research): two of three of oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology. All three are present, and thyroid, prolactin, and CAH have been excluded. (Source: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

options.text: Request an MRI of the adrenals and ovaries to exclude an androgen-secreting tumour
options.rationale: Imaging for androgen-secreting tumours is indicated when testosterone is grossly elevated or virilisation is present. Neither applies here. (Source: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

options.text: Defer the diagnosis pending a 2-hour 75 g oral glucose tolerance test
options.rationale: OGTT is recommended after the diagnosis to screen for impaired glucose tolerance and type 2 diabetes, especially with raised BMI. It is part of follow-up workup, not a diagnostic criterion. (Source: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS)
source_refs: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

explanation.summary: PCOS is diagnosed by the Rotterdam criteria: two of three of oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. The 2023 Australian-led International Evidence Based Guideline raised the follicle count threshold to over 20 per ovary on high-frequency transvaginal scans, reflecting modern imaging. Thyroid disease, hyperprolactinaemia, and non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia must be excluded first. After diagnosis, screen for impaired glucose tolerance with a 75 g OGTT and assess cardiovascular and endometrial risk.
key_points: Rotterdam: two of three of oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic morphology
2023 morphology threshold: over 20 follicles per ovary on high-frequency transvaginal scan
Exclude thyroid disease, hyperprolactinaemia, and non-classical CAH before diagnosis
After diagnosis: OGTT for IGT/T2DM and endometrial risk assessment
explanation.pearls: PCOS by Rotterdam: two of three of oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic morphology (over 20 follicles per ovary on high-frequency transvaginal scan). Exclude thyroid, prolactin, and CAH first; then OGTT for metabolic screening.

sources.label: 2023 International Evidence Based Guideline for PCOS

stem:
A 23-year-old woman attends with painful periods that have worsened over the past two years. The pain starts the day before her period, peaks on day 2, and stops her attending university for 1 to 2 days each cycle. She also has deep pain during intercourse. She has never been pregnant and is not currently planning pregnancy; she uses condoms for contraception.

General and pelvic examination are unremarkable. There is no adnexal tenderness on bimanual examination and no nodularity is felt on rectovaginal palpation. She has tried ibuprofen with limited effect. She has no contraindications to combined hormonal contraception (no smoking history, no migraine with aura, no thrombotic risk factors, normal blood pressure).

data_table.Examination: normal vulva and vagina, soft non-tender cervix, no adnexal mass, no rectovaginal nodularity
data_table.Investigations: transvaginal ultrasound: normal-sized anteverted uterus, no endometrioma, ovaries normal volume, no deep infiltrating lesions identified

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy as the first management step
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is no longer required to label endometriosis-related pain. Empirical medical therapy is recommended first under RANZCOG and ESHRE; laparoscopy is reserved for treatment failure or suspected deep infiltrating disease. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Trial a continuous combined oral contraceptive pill plus regular ibuprofen
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology endorse empirical medical therapy for suspected endometriosis-related pain when imaging is normal, particularly in younger women not seeking pregnancy. Continuous (skipping the placebo week) combined hormonal contraception is first-line. Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for failure of empirical treatment or features suggestive of deep disease. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Commence a 3-month course of GnRH agonist with hormonal add-back therapy
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second or third line after failure of combined hormonal contraception and progestogens. They have bone-density and menopausal-symptom trade-offs and need add-back therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Reassure her that primary dysmenorrhoea is benign and review in 12 months
options.rationale: Pain that stops her attending university and is associated with deep dyspareunia is not consistent with simple primary dysmenorrhoea. RANZCOG recommends active treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Order serum CA-125 to confirm or exclude an endometriosis diagnosis
options.rationale: CA-125 is non-specific and not used for diagnosis or exclusion. It may be elevated in endometriosis but also in many other conditions and is not part of the RANZCOG diagnostic pathway. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG and ESHRE allow empirical medical management of suspected endometriosis without surgical confirmation, particularly for younger women not currently seeking pregnancy. Continuous combined hormonal contraception is the recommended first-line, with progestogen-only options (oral, depot, or LNG-IUS) as alternatives if combined contraception is contraindicated. NSAIDs are adjunctive. Laparoscopy is reserved for failure of medical therapy, features of deep infiltrating disease, or fertility-driven assessment. CA-125 is not part of the diagnostic pathway.
key_points: Empirical continuous combined hormonal contraception is first-line
Progestogen-only options if combined contraception is contraindicated
Laparoscopy for failure of medical therapy or suspected deep infiltrating disease
CA-125 is non-diagnostic; transvaginal ultrasound for endometriomas and DIE only
explanation.pearls: Suspected endometriosis-related pain in a young non-pregnancy-seeking woman: empirical continuous combined hormonal contraception plus NSAIDs first-line. Laparoscopy only after failed medical therapy or features of deep infiltrating disease.

stem:
A 62-year-old woman attends your practice with a single episode of light vaginal bleeding three days ago, the first since her menopause at age 53. She is otherwise well, takes no medication, has never used hormone replacement therapy, and is not on tamoxifen. Her BMI is 34 and she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin.

On examination there is no obvious vulval or vaginal lesion, the cervix appears healthy, and bimanual examination is unremarkable. There is no active bleeding at the time of review. Cervical screening is up to date and negative.

data_table.Examination: atrophic vulva and vagina, healthy cervix, no adnexal mass, no rectovaginal nodularity
data_table.Investigations: transvaginal ultrasound: endometrial thickness 7 mm with irregular endomyometrial junction

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound in 3 months for surveillance
options.rationale: Once the threshold is exceeded, the next step is tissue sampling, not surveillance imaging. Delay risks progression of an underlying neoplasm. (Source: RANZCOG Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Reassure that one episode of bleeding does not require investigation
options.rationale: Any episode of postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation under RANZCOG, regardless of volume or whether it recurs. Endometrial cancer can present with a single bleed. (Source: RANZCOG Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Commence vaginal oestrogen for atrophy and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats atrophic vaginitis but does not address the suspicious endometrial finding. Empirical treatment without sampling risks missing endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial thickness over 4 mm on transvaginal ultrasound exceeds the RANZCOG threshold to proceed to endometrial sampling. The combination of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and unopposed oestrogen exposure further raises endometrial cancer risk. Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is the diagnostic standard. (Source: RANZCOG Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Order an MRI pelvis to characterise the endometrium before any sampling
options.rationale: MRI is used to stage known endometrial cancer or assess myometrial invasion, not as a primary diagnostic test. Tissue sampling comes first. (Source: RANZCOG Investigation of Postmenopausal Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation. Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line test, with an endometrial thickness over 4 mm prompting endometrial sampling. Obesity, diabetes, polycystic ovary syndrome, tamoxifen use, unopposed oestrogen, and Lynch syndrome are major risk factors that lower the threshold for early biopsy. Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is preferred over blind pipelle when focal lesions or polyps are suspected.
key_points: Any episode of postmenopausal bleeding needs investigation
TVUS endometrial thickness over 4 mm: proceed to endometrial sampling
Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is preferred when focal pathology is suspected
Obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, and tamoxifen are major risk factors
explanation.pearls: Postmenopausal bleeding: TVUS first; endometrial thickness over 4 mm prompts hysteroscopy and biopsy. Obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, and tamoxifen lower the threshold.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Endometrial Cancer Statement

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 34+2 weeks is referred from her shared-care GP to the birthing unit with a 24-hour history of headache and visual flashing lights. Her booking blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg. On arrival now her readings are repeatedly elevated and she has hyperreflexia with three beats of ankle clonus.

Fetal CTG shows a normal baseline with reassuring variability and accelerations. Her urine dipstick is 3+ for protein and the spot urine protein-creatinine ratio is 84 mg/mmol (less than 30). She is for admission with senior obstetric and anaesthetic input. She has had no seizures and is GCS 15. The midwife asks what to commence first.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 92 /min, blood pressure 168/112 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: hyperreflexia, three beats of clonus, headache with visual sparkles, no focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: platelets 142 x10^9/L (150 to 400); ALT 78 U/L (5 to 35); creatinine 88 micromol/L (45 to 90); urate 0.42 mmol/L; spot UPCR 84 mg/mmol; CTG reassuring

lead_in: What is the single best initial intervention?

options.text: Withhold magnesium given the borderline creatinine and observe closely
options.rationale: Renal impairment is not a contraindication to magnesium; it is a reason to adjust the maintenance infusion and monitor magnesium levels and reflexes more closely. Withholding seizure prophylaxis in this setting is not safe. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg bolus and proceed to delivery within 30 minutes
options.rationale: BP control with labetalol or hydralazine is essential but is concurrent with magnesium, and immediate delivery is not always required at 34+2 weeks with reassuring CTG. Steroid cover for fetal lung maturity and stabilisation precede delivery in this window. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: IV phenytoin 15 mg/kg loading dose over 30 minutes for seizure prophylaxis
options.rationale: The Magpie trial established magnesium as superior to phenytoin and diazepam for eclampsia prevention. Phenytoin is no longer recommended for this indication. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Oral nifedipine 20 mg immediate release stat and review in one hour
options.rationale: Nifedipine is a valid BP-lowering option but does not prevent seizures and is not the single best initial intervention with this constellation of cerebral irritation features. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g per hour infusion
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with cerebral features (headache, visual disturbance, hyperreflexia, clonus) carries imminent eclampsia risk. The RANZCOG and SOMANZ standard is magnesium sulfate IV 4 g over 20 minutes then 1 g per hour for 24 hours, with reflex and respiratory rate monitoring. BP control with labetalol or hydralazine follows but seizure prophylaxis is the immediate priority. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia is defined by SOMANZ as systolic BP at least 160 mmHg or diastolic at least 110 mmHg, or BP elevation plus end-organ involvement (cerebral features, pulmonary oedema, oliguria, raised transaminases, thrombocytopenia, raised creatinine, fetal compromise). Magnesium sulfate IV is the standard seizure prophylaxis (Magpie trial) and is given as a loading dose of 4 g over 20 minutes followed by 1 g per hour for at least 24 hours, with reflex, respiratory rate, and urine output monitoring. Antihypertensive therapy (IV labetalol or hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine) is concurrent. Delivery is the definitive treatment but stabilisation, magnesium, and antenatal corticosteroid cover (if under 35 weeks) precede it where possible.
key_points: Magnesium IV 4 g over 20 min then 1 g per hour for 24 hours is seizure prophylaxis
Magpie trial: magnesium is superior to phenytoin and diazepam for eclampsia prevention
Renal impairment requires dose adjustment and closer monitoring, not omission
BP control and delivery planning are concurrent priorities, not substitutes for magnesium
explanation.pearls: Severe pre-eclampsia with cerebral features: IV magnesium sulfate 4 g over 20 minutes then 1 g per hour for 24 hours. Concurrent BP control with labetalol or hydralazine; antenatal corticosteroids if under 35 weeks.
